speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Lyon, why did you choose to give a testimony to the survivors of the Shoah Visual History Project, in light of all the other speaking engagements and books in which you have appeared?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Shoah foundation is doing very important work. It has always, always been my very strong conviction that the oral histories of survivors must be given while we are here. It must not die with us. I feel that what the Shoah Foundation is doing is number one on my list. I must convince all survivors I meet, each survivor I meet, that his or her oral history story must be given so that future generations, and scholars who study our history, must learn from it. This is not just a Jewish story. This is a human story. We must learn what not to do to each other as human beings. And we must learn to live with each other on this small planet, Earth. Now, I have told you a very intimate part of my life, and by now, it is a part of recorded history. So if I may close with these words. These days we talk much about racism, often too lightly. The Holocaust which annihilated the Jewish communities of Europe was pure and simple racism in its most horrible and tragic form. Well, it cannot be dismissed as the brainchild of that one madman, Hitler. No, rather it was the climax of many centuries of hatred and prejudice and discrimination against a people which had contributed so much to civilization. In some way, to some degree, it can happen again, anywhere, anytime, and to any people, any race or color, unless-- unless we are constantly on guard to eradicate hatred and prejudice and discrimination among all human beings. We must treat each other more gently and humanely. We are but a handful of survivors, and we have a sacred obligation to bear witness. And when we are gone, we hope that those who hear my-- our story-- mine and all other survivors-- will remember the message of this story. I thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Lyon, on behalf of the survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation, I thank you. We thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I especially want to thank Mr. Steven Spielberg, whose films I love, for making this possible for the world. And I want to personally thank you, Mr Spielberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Lyon, again, thank you very much for your kind testimony. I feel that we would like to speak about something of great import that very few people knew about. And that's why we're using this time as an addendum to your testimony. And that is the subject of gypsies in the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Certainly. How could I have forgotten that? Every day, as we walked to work in Auschwitz-Birkenau, we passed by the gypsy camp. This is the only time that we saw little babies, children of different ages, with their parents. Men, women, and children were together. And as the morning sun would touch the side of the barrack, there they sat along the side of the barrack. And little babies were suckling their mothers' breasts. And these--these people who were so thin, emaciated, even-- I'm sure that that child, or those little babies, didn't get much out of it but just love and affection. But we-- we always looked forward to passing by the gypsy camp just to see babies, just to reassure us that the human race is not really disappearing.

speaker: interviewee
text: This went on for months. I was in Auschwitz for about seven and 1/2 months. And one day, the gypsy camp was totally silent. We didn't see anybody. No movement whatsoever. And we were wondering, what happened? We thought of the worst, of course. Then, again, a day or two later, the news reached us that everyone in the gypsy camp was annihilated in the gas chambers. I don't think that there was a dry eye in the camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was almost like we were wrong to hope that some children remained alive. Alive. Thank you very much. Would you please introduce your husband, Karl Lyon?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Karl Lyon, of 47 years and a few months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would like to say that I'm very proud of Gloria. She has done a great deal with the unfortunate experiences that she had to live through. For the last 20 years, she has gone out and spoken to many thousands of people, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] telling them her history. Not because it's her history, but as an example of what can happen when human beings behave toward each other the way the Nazis behaved toward the Jews. It's an object lesson for all humanity. That's why we also made the documentary film about her life, which will carry on her work when she is no longer able to do it. Because even though she is one of the youngest of survivors, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there will come a time when she will not be able to do it anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the meantime, she is continuing her work. And I'm very glad that the Shoah Foundation took her oral history. It's of significance universally. And hopefully, human beings will learn something from her work, and from the work of the Shoah Foundation.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my maternal grandfather, Herman Gelb-- G-E-L-B. He was born in Austria-Hungary. And I don't know the exact date of his birth, but he died in NagyBereg-- N-a-g-y-B-e-r-e-g-- in October, 1930. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my mother, Helene Gelb Hollander. She lived through Auschwitz, and she died three years after liberation, in 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was 21 years old when this was taken. Was born in 1895.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: These two gentlemen are my father, David Hollander, and his brother, Farkas Hollander. F-A-R-K-A-S. They were Jewish soldiers in the Austrian-Hungarian Army in World War I. My father was decorated for bravery in action, sitting down. In World War II, my dad's medals were torn away from him, and he was imprisoned in Auschwitz and Dora-Mittelbau. My dad survived. Uncle Farkas went directly into the gas chambers. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] From left to right, adults in back row. Rosa Gelb, my maternal grandmother, formerly Rote. Then my Aunt Violet, and my uncle-- mother's brother-- Adolph Gero. G-E-R-O. They came from St. Louis, Missouri to-- for this occasion. To the right of the gravestone is my mother, who is pregnant with my sister, Annuska, and to-- my mother, Helen Hollander. And to her right, my dad-- who just came from the fields-- David Hollander. In the front row is my brother, Sandor Hollander. And to his right, Viktor Hollander. And in the far right, Michael Hollander, my three-- three of my four brothers. Sitting on the gravestone, on the left side, is me, Gloria Hollander Lyon, at that time, Heinle Hollander. And to my right, my first cousin, Judith Gero, my uncle's and aunt's child. We are 20 days apart. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my uncle, Adolph Gero. G-E-R-O. He was born in NagyBereg. During this period, he studied in Budapest, Hungary. At that time, it belonged to Czechoslovakia. I'm sorry, he is from Czechoslovakia, while he was in Budapest, Hungary. This is my mother's sister, Leonie Gelb Parnass. P-A-R-N-A-S-S. She was born in Austria-Hungary, and emigrated to the United States. Died in New York City. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] To the left is my uncle, mother's brother, Adolph Gero, formerly Gelb. And next to him, in the middle, is his wife. To the right is my mother's sister, Bella Yagoda, formerly Gelb. They were American citizens for many years. My uncle Adolph and my Aunt Bella, on this picture, brought me to the United States in 1947. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is what my family looked like just before I was born. It could well be that my mother is pregnant, on this picture, with me. To the left is my oldest brother, Joszef Hollander, who died in an industrial accident at the [INAUDIBLE], where years-- several years later, we were interned in the barrack-- in the ghetto-- in the ghetto barracks as. In the far right is my brother, Michael Hollander. And to the left, in the first row, is my brother Sandor Hollander. And sitting in my mother's lap is Viktor Hollander, who-- whom we lost in Auschwitz-Birkenau in 1945, three days before liberation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture indicates Jewish enrollment in the community. There were two Jewish men in this picture that I recognize. My father is in the first row, far right, with an X above his head. And our neighbor, Joseph Neifeld, is below the other X on top. This was during the period of Czechoslovakia when being a Jew was an honor. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Annuska and I, two sisters, one year before the Holocaust, in NagyBereg, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am the taller one. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I love this picture. And it makes me sad. When my mother learned that I stayed alive in Sweden, and was in Sweden, she made sure that I get a picture of my two brothers and sister who came home from Auschwitz. To the right is Michael Hollander, to the left is Sandor Hollander. And the young lady is my sister, Annuska. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture makes me sad, because it shows my mother after Auschwitz as a broken down woman. There she lost her upper teeth. And she is back home in NagyBereg shortly before her death. She sent me this picture before she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here I am, Gloria Hollander Lyon-- oh, I'm sorry, just Heinle Hollander yet-- in 1945. This is my first picture after liberation, while I was still in quarantine. It was taken by the Swedish family who wanted to adopt me later, about-- across the fence. I still have open wounds on my legs, which would not heal for months. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] These two lovely people nurtured me back to good health in Sweden. They are my foster parents, who took me in and gave me lots of love and good food, Fabror Erik and Tant Lily, Eric Berglund and Lily Berglund in Landskrona, Sweden. This was taken a few years after I came to the United States. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Dinner with my Swedish family at my first reunion with them after my visit in Lviv with my real family. On my way back, this picture was taken in Landskrona, Sweden. To the left is my foster mother, Lily Berglund, my foster father, Erik Berglund, my Swedish sister, Gulan Berglund Aaronson. To her-- to her right is my Swedish brother-in-law, Stieg Aaronson. And-- and me, at the age of 32, in 1962. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At my very first reunion with my real family, in Lviv, USSR, in 1962. My brother, Sandor-- who was off somewhere in one of the states in the Soviet Union-- received my telegram that I am coming. And he left his job to come and meet me in Lviv. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, David Hollander, shortly after we brought him out of the Soviet Union. The picture was taken in San Francisco, California, USA. I love that twinkle in his eye. Don't you? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We took my father to Israel on his-- which was his lifelong wish, in 1977, where he met Golda Meir and was invited to the Knesset with us for a celebration and met his grandson and his future wife, where they got engaged. He gave them his blessings, and we parted. Dad, Karl, and I took a plane to come back to San Francisco. About 200 miles from Canada, my father dropped dead in mid-air. His fellow passengers immediately collected money to buy 100 trees to be planted in his memory in Israel, a garden of 100 trees. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Gloria Hollander Lyon at Ocean Beach, California, age 20. In San Francisco. I always loved the bicycles. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That's me, Gloria Hollander Lyon, with my two sons, David, born in 1953, and Jonathan, in 1955. I believe the picture was taken-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think he was five years old. They were five years old and three years old, respectively. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In 1963. My family, in 1960-- I forget. Well, Karl, Jonathan, Gloria, and David. I'm not certain about the year. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My son David and daughter-in-law Iris Lyon-- formerly Messica-- at their marriage in Rome, Italy at the Sephardic synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's from Libya? She's from Libya?

speaker: interviewee
text: Iris was born in Libya, and at the age of 13, she fled with her family to Italy, for it wasn't safe in Libya any more for a Jewish family. And David met her at a dance. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] These three grandchildren of ours [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were-- live in Modesto, California. They are all Americans. The are the children of our oldest son David, and his wife Iris. To the left is Deborah Guela Lyon. In the middle is Ronit Yael Lyon. And to the right is Ilana Rachel Lyon. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture of my younger son Jonathan's family, Jonathan Victor Lyon, MS, younger son of Gloria Hollander Lyon and Karl D. Lyon. His wife, Desmid Bendix Lyon. And their five children-- Shia, Benjamin, Joachim, Miriam, and Samuel. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My sister, Annuska, visited me in San Francisco. And she proceeded to teach me how to make challah in our old kitchen. I loved it. She learned it from my mother, whereas I had to learn how to cook from books. It's quite refreshing to learn it from her. A little bit of this and a little bit of that, and the challah comes out perfectly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my sister, Annuska, who lost her husband four years before. And she remarried Isador Rosenbaum. So she called me, and asked me to come to her wedding. Present were her three children-- Judith, Leah, and her son, their spouses, and their children, and grandchildren, in Brooklyn, New York. This document is part of my research about the date that I arrived in Bergen-Belsen from Auschwitz-Birkenau. According to the International Red Cross in Arolsen, Germany, I arrived in Bergen-Belsen at the end of 1944. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a document from Wiedergutmachung in Trier, Germany, which is a settlement for having been incarcerated in the concentration camps, which they could verify listed under it. The KL-- the KL, rather-- indicates konzentrationslager, or concentration camps. A sum of 1800 German marks-- or deutschmarks-- was awarded to me. And this was altogether the sum that I received from Germany. A one-time sum of 1800 deutschmarks, which at that time totaled approximately $350, $360. This is the original document that I found at the Yad Vashem archives while researching there. I filled it out right after I arrived in Sweden. And on these pages, it indicates the person most likely to have stayed alive. On the other page, it gives my brother's name. And I thought that not having come to Auschwitz with us, but having gone to slave labor, that he's most likely to have survived the Holocaust. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Gloria Hollander Lyon is giving a lecture on the Holocaust-- on her Holocaust history at Milpitas High School, in Milpitas, California, on November 29, 1989. The students have always wanted to look at my tattoo, after having heard my story to see how we have become untermenschen in the eyes of the SS. This is after having listened to an hour lecture. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Dr. Fullberg-Stolberg in Hanover, Germany, has written my story in this book, Women in Concentration Camp-- Bergen-Belsen and Ravensbruck. It can be obtained from Temin Publishers [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This book contains my story. In fact, the longest story in the book is my story. Photojournalist Marie Wada from Japan interviewed me for my story, for the testimony of the 20th century, before and after the Berlin Wall. Gloria Hollander Lyon is pointing out to Marie Wada the point, or the place, where I was taken with my family to Auschwitz-Birkenau. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I appear in this book, also, published in 1989 by Collins Publishers-- now HarperCollins Publishers. This is-- this picture appears in which was released for the cover of a videotape which was made in a high school in California as I told the students my story. A-6374, The Story of a Holocaust-- of a Holocaus Survivor, can also be purchased at the Holocaust Museum bookshop in Washington, D.C. The black and white case is the documentary film, recently finished. It's a professional film which also tells my story.
